era_context: The late 12th century CE saw Saladin's rise to power in the Islamic world, the decline of the Crusader states, and the High Middle Ages in Europe. The Jin Dynasty controlled northern China while the Song Dynasty held the south. The Khmer Empire was flourishing under strong leadership. Japan was in the early Kamakura period, with the Genpei War either beginning or about to begin.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond major kingdoms

events.title: Saladin defeats the Zengid dynasty; consolidates Ayyubid power
events.description: Saladin defeated the Zengid dynasty (the previous rulers of Syria) and consolidated Ayyubid control over Syria and Mesopotamia. This victory was crucial in Saladin's rise to power and his unification of the Muslim world. By eliminating the Zengids, Saladin removed a major rival and created a unified Islamic state that could effectively challenge the Crusader presence in the Levant. This consolidation was a prerequisite for his later campaigns against the Crusaders.

sources.name: Baha al-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Saladin's defeat of the Zengids is well-documented in Islamic sources, but the precise dating of individual campaigns is approximate. The consolidation occurred over several years (1174–1176); 1175 is within this period.
cross_references: 1187-001 (Fall of Jerusalem, culmination of Saladin's consolidation)

events.title: Henry II of England and Philip II of France conflict over Angevin domains
events.description: King Henry II of England and King Philip II of France were in conflict over control of the Angevin domains in France. This conflict would intensify over the following years and eventually lead to the loss of most of the Angevin territories by Henry's successors. The struggle between English and French kings for control of French territories was a defining feature of the late 12th and 13th centuries and would eventually lead to the Hundred Years' War.
events.certainty_note: The conflict between Henry II and Philip II is well-documented in English and French chronicles. The specific events of 1175 are less detailed than major battles, but the general conflict is confirmed.

events.title: Genpei War begins in Japan; Minamoto-Taira conflict
events.description: The Genpei War between the Minamoto and Taira clans either began in 1175 or was in its early stages. The war would last until 1185, with the Minamoto achieving final victory at the Battle of Dan-no-ura. The conflict was transforming Japanese political structure, moving power from the imperial court to the military aristocracy. The war's outcome would establish the Kamakura shogunate and the samurai-dominated feudal system.
events.certainty_note: The Genpei War's chronology is debated among scholars. The war is traditionally dated 1180–1185, but some sources suggest earlier conflicts. The dating of 1175 is speculative; the war likely began in 1180.

events.title: Jayavarman VII's reign in the Khmer Empire (approximate)
events.description: Jayavarman VII was ruling the Khmer Empire during this period (his reign is traditionally dated 1181–1219, though some sources suggest earlier dates). He was one of the greatest builder-kings of the Khmer Empire, constructing the Bayon temple complex and expanding Angkor Thom. His reign represented the peak of Khmer power and artistic achievement, with extensive temple construction, road networks, and hospital systems reflecting both religious devotion and administrative sophistication.

sources.name: Bayon temple inscriptions and architectural analysis
Zhou Daguan, Records of Cambodia (13th century account)

events.certainty_note: Jayavarman VII's reign is traditionally dated 1181–1219, so 1175 may predate his reign. The entry reflects the broader context of Khmer power during this period. The dating is approximate (±5 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The Genpei War's chronology is disputed. The war is traditionally dated 1180–1185, which would place 1175 in the pre-war period. Some sources suggest earlier conflicts, but 1175 is likely too early for the major war. The entry has been framed as approximate to reflect this uncertainty. Jayavarman VII's reign is traditionally dated 1181–1219, so 1175 may predate his reign. The entry reflects the broader context rather than definitively attributing events to Jayavarman VII.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1175 is moderate for Western Europe and the Islamic world, sparse for East Asia, and minimal for other regions. Islamic sources on Saladin are abundant but often compiled after his death. English and French chronicles provide good documentation of Henry II and Philip II's conflict. Japanese sources are later literary compilations with inherent dating uncertainties. Khmer sources are primarily epigraphic and architectural, limiting narrative detail. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia are essentially undocumented for this year.

graph_edges.note: Saladin's consolidation of power in 1175 enabled his conquest of Jerusalem in 1187
The conflict between Henry II and Philip II over Angevin domains would intensify and lead to territorial losses
The Genpei War (if it began in 1175 or earlier) led to the decisive Minamoto victory at Dan-no-ura
